card.intuition: Agglomerative (bottom-up): start with each point as its own cluster; repeatedly merge the two closest clusters until one remains. The result is a dendrogram — a tree of all merges. Cut the dendrogram at a height to get k clusters.
card.analogy: Organising books by topic: start by grouping the two most similar books, then group the closest pairs of groups, continuing until everything is in one library. The merge history is the hierarchy — cut at any level to get a different number of groupings.
card.time_complexity: O(n³) naïve; O(n² log n) with priority queue
when_to_use: When you want a hierarchy of clusters (not just a flat partition)
No need to specify k in advance — examine the dendrogram to choose
Phylogenetic trees, document clustering, gene expression analysis
gotchas: Linkage affects cluster shape: single (chaining effect), complete (compact spheres), average/Ward (most commonly used)
Does not scale to large n — pairwise distances require O(n²) space
Ward linkage minimises within-cluster variance — often the best choice for compact clusters

exercise.prompt: Implement agglomerative hierarchical clustering with single linkage.
hints: After merging merge_b into merge_a, update labels for all points that are now in merge_a's group: iterate over points_in[merge_a] (which now includes the merged points)
exercise.test_code:
X = [[1,1],[1.2,0.8],[5,5],[5.2,4.8],[1,5],[1.2,4.8]]
labels = single_linkage_clustering(X, n_clusters=3)
# Points 0,1 should be in one cluster; 2,3 in another; 4,5 in another
assert labels[0] == labels[1], 'Points 0,1 should cluster together'
assert labels[2] == labels[3], 'Points 2,3 should cluster together'
assert labels[4] == labels[5], 'Points 4,5 should cluster together'
assert len(set(labels)) == 3
print('PASS')
